notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
As a self-employed UX consultant in Austin, navigating the individual health insurance market requires balancing your need for tax-safe cash flow with the reality of uneven income. Since your records show a $415 monthly payment to HealthCare.gov and a $300 contribution to a Fidelity HSA, you are already utilizing the primary tools available to independent professionals in 2026.
Professional expertise disclaimer
Since insurance and tax planning involve specialized expertise, I'll explain the concepts educationally. Per CFP Board standards, consult a licensed insurance professional and a qualified tax advisor for advice specific to your situation before making decisions.
Health insurance selection factors
When reviewing your marketplace options for the 2026 plan year, three primary variables will impact your finances:
The subsidy cliff: Enhanced federal subsidies for premiums are currently scheduled to expire. Unless extended, individuals with higher freelance earnings could see significant increases in net monthly premiums. Since you had a strong income month in May of $13,960, tracking your total annual earnings is critical to estimating your final tax credit eligibility.
Out-of-pocket maximums: For 2026, the federal maximum out-of-pocket limit has increased to $10,600 for individuals. This is your "worst-case scenario" cost for in-network care and should be the minimum amount you aim to keep in your cash reserve or emergency fund.
Network restrictions: In the Austin market, insurers frequently use narrow health maintenance organization (HMO) networks to control costs. I'd suggest confirming that your providers, such as the Austin Therapy Group you visited in May, remain in-network for any new plan you consider.
HSA eligibility and 2026 rules
Your $300 monthly HSA contribution is a fantastic way to build a triple-tax-advantaged safety net. In 2026, the landscape for health savings accounts has shifted favorably for marketplace shoppers:
Bronze and catastrophic plan compatibility: Under 2026 regulations, all bronze and catastrophic marketplace plans are treated as HSA-eligible high deductible health plans (HDHPs). This removes much of the administrative guesswork previously required to confirm eligibility.
Contribution limits: The 2026 individual contribution limit is $4,400. At your current rate of $300 per month ($3,600 annually), you have approximately $800 of remaining "room" to contribute before the end of the year if your cash flow allows.
First-dollar coverage: Ensure your plan doesn't provide "disqualifying" coverage, such as a general-purpose flexible spending account (FSA) or a non-HDHP secondary plan, which would make you ineligible to contribute to your HSA.
Self-employed tax considerations
Since you file a Schedule C as a consultant, the way you deduct these costs is unique. Your health insurance premiums and HSA contributions are "above-the-line" deductions that reduce your adjusted gross income (AGI) and federal income tax.
However, a critical distinction is that these deductions do not reduce your self-employment tax. While business expenses like your $350 WeWork rent or $186 office supplies reduce the earnings subject to self-employment tax, your personal health premiums and HSA deposits generally do not.
Would you like to see how much more you could potentially contribute to your HSA this year to maximize your tax savings? 📈
